title: OnlyFans Models Are Accidentally Making Hacked Government Websites Disappear
gist: UpGuard research shows that DMCA copyright takedowns filed for adult creators have inadvertently exposed and cleaned up thousands of compromised .gov and .edu sites, revealing a booming creator anti-piracy industry, insecure government web infrastructure, and questions about DMCA overreach.

insights.claim: More than 2,000 government and education domains across 80 countries have received adult-creator copyright takedown requests over 15 years, an indicator the sites were compromised, per UpGuard analysis shared with WIRED.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Quantifies pervasive insecurity in official web infrastructure exploitable for SEO scams.
insights.quote: More than 2,000 domains belonging to governments and education institutions, across 80 countries, have received copyright takedown requests linked to adult content creators over the past 15 years

insights.claim: UpGuard counted 384,286 adult-creator takedown requests covering 631,193 URLs to government and education sites since 2011, of which Google removed roughly 130,000 URLs while taking no action on 460,000.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Shows most flagged malicious URLs on official domains remain live in Google's index.
insights.quote: there have been 384,286 takedown requests, covering 631,193 URLs, from adult content creators to government and education websites since 2011.
insights.locator: Pollock's analysis says there have been

insights.claim: Hijackings of official sites tied to individual adult creators' leaked OnlyFans content rose dramatically since 2020, with compromised .gov and .edu pages exploited because they rank high in Google results.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: The authority of .gov/.edu domains is itself the attack surface being monetized by scammers.
insights.quote: a “dramatic” increase in hijackings related to individual adult creators and their “leaked” OnlyFans content since 2020
insights.locator: Many of the sites have been repeatedly compromised

insights.claim: Scammers hijack authoritative .gov and .edu domains to upload pages baiting searches for free movies, iPhones, porn, and Fortnite skins, then redirect victims to scam or malware pages, increasingly using adult creators' names as the lure.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Details the SEO-poisoning funnel that turns trusted domains into fraud infrastructure.
insights.quote: A compromised .gov page ranking for a trending creator's name is a near-perfect funnel
insights.locator: For years, scammers have hijacked official websites

insights.claim: Because the malicious asset has no visibility outside Google, getting Google to remove the search result is extremely effective at neutralizing these hijack scams, per UpGuard's Greg Pollock.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Search delisting functions as a de facto security control, elevating Google's gatekeeper role.
insights.quote: having Google remove the search result is extremely effective, because there's no real visibility of the asset outside of Google.
insights.locator: The OnlyFans models are not setting out to help

insights.claim: A whole industry of DMCA-service firms now polices creator piracy, with Lux calling anti-piracy filing essentially mandatory because stolen content is otherwise 'a Google search away.'
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: The adult creator economy sustains a growing content-protection services market.
insights.quote: If you are not running a DMCA service, then you might as well probably not even be bothering doing the job, because it will be everywhere
insights.locator: Content creators have filed millions of requests

insights.claim: Estonia-based Rulta has driven the surge, making around 90 percent of adult-content takedown requests to government and education sites in recent years, among 11,000 copyright owners represented by 554 organizations.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Names the dominant player concentrating this anti-piracy filing activity.
insights.quote: It has made around 90 percent of the requests in the past couple of years
insights.locator: The surge in requests to government and education websites

insights.claim: Experts argue copyright law is the wrong tool for cleaning hacked official sites, with Ceartas CEO Dan Purcell and Fanlock's Alexander Small saying pages using a creator's name as bait without the actual content are not copyright violations.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Flags legal-abuse risk in DMCA takedowns extending beyond genuine copyright grounds.
insights.quote: copyright laws are the wrong tool for cleaning up hacked official sites.
insights.locator: Purcell says the company has seen compromised

insights.claim: Monitoring for popular models' names can serve as an early-warning breach signal for under-resourced security teams, since injected unwanted content is often catchable via adult-content keywords, per UpGuard's Pollock.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Offers a cheap detection heuristic for compromised web infrastructure.
insights.quote: When that unwanted content is injected, you can often catch it with these kinds of adult content keywords.
insights.locator: Monitoring for the names of popular models

insights.claim: The DMCA has long attracted complainants with SEO, privacy, and other non-copyright grievances, and using it outside copyright makes the notice legally questionable, per UC Berkeley law professor Jennifer Urban.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Highlights structural weakness in the 1998 DMCA regime that invites misuse.
insights.quote: The DMCA has long been attractive for complainants who have SEO, privacy, and other issues with content or links posted online
insights.locator: The DMCA has long been attractive
